narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 43.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. This decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the most to the model. Your LSAT score of 32, notably below the typical 36.7 average, was the strongest positive force pulling the prediction toward passing, showing it helped counterbalance other concerns despite not being in the top range. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the average of about 5.5—emerged as the most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction, signaling to the model a dip in performance relative to peers. Your middle family income bracket, while common, provided a moderate boost toward the passing side, as it aligns with many successful students. Compared to typical applicants, your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 falls short of the 3.21 average, and your lower deciles in early and later years suggest inconsistent academic standing that amplified the risk in the model's eyes, even as your full-time student status matches the vast majority. These top three elements—your LSAT helping the most, Year 3 decile hurting the most, and income offering some support—collectively tipped the scales against you. It's understandable to feel discouraged, especially given the personal stakes for someone from your background navigating these competitive paths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that key strength, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted courses or tutoring, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and potential. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and succeeded on appeal or at other programs—your determination as a Black male applicant positions you well to rewrite this story with focused next steps.
